label: Title/Abstract
verbatim: we revisit the existing SVD-based compression method and propose D-Rank, a framework with layer-wise balanced Dynamic Rank allocation for LLMs compression. We first introduce effective rank as a principled metric to measure the information density of weight matrices, and then allocate ranks via a Lagrange multiplier-based optimization scheme to adaptively assign more capacity to groups with higher information density under a fixed compression ratio. Moreover, we rebalance the allocated ranks across attention layers to account for their varying importance and extend D-Rank to latest LLMs with grouped-query attention.
paraphrase: D-Rank: layer-wise dynamic rank allocation using effective rank (spectral entropy) under Lagrange multipliers, with attention rebalancing and GQA extension.
preconditions: SVD-based post-training LLM compression; calibration activations available
consequences: Defines the rank-budget allocation Stage 3 of the spec relies on

label: Abstract
verbatim: achieving more than 15 lower perplexity on the C4 dataset with LLaMA-3-8B at 20% compression ratio and up to 5% higher zero-shot reasoning accuracy with LLaMA-7B at 40% compression ratio, while also delivering higher throughput.
paraphrase: Headline results: ≥15 PPL gain on C4 LLaMA-3-8B@20%; up to +5% zero-shot acc LLaMA-7B@40%.
preconditions: Comparison vs Basis Sharing / SVD-LLM baselines
consequences: Reference numbers spec may cite for D-Rank performance

label: §3.1 Preliminary
verbatim: We can first concatenate matrices within the same group horizontally: W = [W(1) W(2) ... W(n)] ∈ R^{d1×(nd2)}. We then perform SVD: W = UΣV⊤. After truncating to the top k singular values, we obtain: W ≈ Wk = UkΣkV⊤k
paraphrase: Per-group horizontal concatenation followed by truncated SVD with shared basis B=UkΣk and per-layer coefficient blocks C^(i).
preconditions: n layers per group; same input dim d1; equal d2 per layer
consequences: Defines the n=group_size grouping spec inherits from Basis Sharing

label: §3.1 Whitening
verbatim: we can incorporate the input activation statistical information S for SVD calculation, which can be expressed as SS⊤ = cholesky(X⊤X) and W = S^{-1}(SW). Following these works, we apply SVD to the scaled matrix SW instead of W: SW ≈ U′kΣ′kV′⊤k, and we can reconstruct W as W ≈ S^{-1} U′kΣ′kV′⊤k = B′′C′
paraphrase: Whitening via Cholesky of X⊤X; SVD applied to SW; reconstruction multiplies S^{-1} back. Standard Basis-Sharing/SVD-LLM whitening.
preconditions: Calibration activations X; X⊤X positive-definite Cholesky factorisation
consequences: Spec must use SS⊤=cholesky(X⊤X) — note S is a Cholesky factor of X⊤X, not (X⊤X)^{1/2}

verbatim: Notably, when n = 1 only, the procedure is the standard SVD-LLM approach.
paraphrase: Setting group size n=1 reduces D-Rank's prelim to SVD-LLM (no cross-layer basis sharing).
consequences: Justifies using D-Rank with n=1 for GQA models (LLaMA-3) without losing the SVD-LLM property

label: Eq. 1
verbatim: p^i_g = λ^i_g / Σ_j λ^j_g
paraphrase: Probability distribution over squared singular values (eigenvalues): p^i_g = σ_i^2 / Σ σ_j^2 for the scaled matrix S_g W_g.
preconditions: λ^i_g = (σ^i_g)^2 squared singular values of S_g W_g; 0 ≤ i < d1
consequences: Defines spectral probability distribution used by Eq. 2 effective rank

label: Eq. 2
verbatim: Reff(g) = exp(- Σ_i p^i_g log p^i_g)
paraphrase: Effective rank = exponential of Shannon entropy of normalized squared singular values; equivalent to perplexity of the spectrum.
preconditions: p^i_g defined via Eq. 1 from σ_i(S_g W_g)^2
consequences: Critical metric for Stage 3 rank budget; spec must compute spectral entropy on whitened matrix S_g W_g, NOT raw W_g

verbatim: We use the effective rank Reff(g) to represent the minimum number of singular values required to effectively represent the uncompressed scaled matrix SgWg. A lower effective rank indicates higher redundancy, while a higher effective rank suggests higher information density of the group.
paraphrase: Reff is interpreted as the effective number of significant singular values; higher Reff = higher information density.
consequences: Justifies allocating more rank to higher-Reff groups

label: Table 1
verbatim: Effective rank of grouped matrices for V, K, Q in LLaMA-7B on Wikitext-2 (two layers as a group): Group 1 V=118 K=6 Q=7; Group 3 V=592 K=8 Q=12; Group 7 V=778 K=12 Q=33; Group 10 V=1026 K=15 Q=24; Group 12 V=973 K=12 Q=25; Group 14 V=1148 K=11 Q=29; Group 16 V=846 K=10 Q=23
paraphrase: Empirically: V matrices have far higher Reff (hundreds–thousands) than K/Q (single–double digits); middle groups higher than ends (U-shape).
consequences: Motivates β-redistribution from Q,K to V

label: §3.2.2 Motivation
verbatim: the middle layers generally have higher effective ranks than the earlier and the later layers, which is consistent with existing studies showing the U-shaped information distribution across depth in Transformer-based models
paraphrase: Effective rank exhibits U-shape across depth: middle layers have higher Reff than ends.
consequences: Layer-wise dynamic rank should preserve this U-shape

label: §3.2.2 ω
verbatim: we denote ω as the parameter cost per rank to represent the number of parameters required to increase the rank of the group by one. For a shared basis, this is calculated as ω = d1 + n d2, where n is the number of layers in the group.
paraphrase: Parameter cost per rank ω = d1 + n·d2 (basis row + n coefficient blocks of width d2). Per-rank parameter accounting for shared-basis groups.
preconditions: Shared-basis grouping with n layers, dims d1×d2 per layer
consequences: Spec must use ω = d1 + n·d2 for shared groups (n>1) and ω = d1 + d2 for ungrouped (n=1)

label: §3.2.2 Tbudget
verbatim: Suppose that the total number of parameters of all groups is T and the target compression ratio is θ, we denote Tbudget = T(1 − θ) = Σ_{g=1} k_g ω as the total number of parameters in the compressed module.
paraphrase: Total budget Tbudget = T(1 − θ) where θ = compression ratio; constraint Σ_g k_g ω_g = Tbudget.
consequences: Spec compression ratio must equal 1 − Σk_g ω_g / T_total

label: Eq. 3
verbatim: minimize_{k1,k2,...} ℓ_total = Σ_{g=1} Reff(g)/k_g  subject to  Σ_{g=1} k_g ω = Tbudget
paraphrase: Optimization: minimize Σ Reff(g)/k_g subject to Σ k_g ω = Tbudget; loss assumed inversely proportional to allocated rank, proportional to Reff.
consequences: Sets up Lagrangian solved in Eqs 4–7 / 13–19

label: Eq. 4
verbatim: F({k_g}, λ) = Σ_{g=1} Reff(g)/k_g + λ (Σ_{g=1} k_g ω − Tbudget)
paraphrase: Lagrangian for the rank-allocation problem.
consequences: Stationary conditions yield Eq. 5

label: Eq. 5
verbatim: ∂F/∂k_g = − Reff(g)/k_g^2 + λ ω = 0
paraphrase: First-order condition for stationary k_g.
consequences: Solved for k_g in Eq. 6

label: Eq. 6
verbatim: k_g ∝ sqrt(Reff(g) / ω)
paraphrase: Optimal rank proportional to √(Reff(g)/ω); higher info density and lower per-rank cost ⇒ more rank.
consequences: Final form in Eq. 7 after applying Tbudget constraint

label: Eq. 7
verbatim: k_g = ( Tbudget / Σ_{j=1} sqrt(Reff(j) ω) ) · ( sqrt(Reff(g)) / sqrt(ω) )
paraphrase: CLOSED-FORM D-RANK ALLOCATION (Eq. 7): k_g = Tbudget · √Reff(g) / ( √ω_g · Σ_j √(Reff(j)·ω_j) ). The exact formula the spec cites for Stage 3 rank budget.
preconditions: Tbudget = T(1−θ); ω = d1 + n d2 per group; Reff(g) computed via Eqs 1-2 on whitened matrix
consequences: CRITICAL: this is the spec's 'Eq. 7' citation for D-Rank rank allocation — must match implementation byte-for-byte

label: §3.2.2 Pipeline
verbatim: Afterwards, we obtain a list L that records the retained rank required for each group of such weight matrices [k1, k2, ..., kG]. ... First, for each group, weight matrices across n layers are concatenated horizontally and multiplied by S to form scaled matrix SgWg ... then we calculate the effective rank of each group of scaled matrix. After we get the rank {k1, k2, ..., kG} for each grouped matrix with Lagrange Multiplier, we will use them as the singular values to perform the SVD compression for every scaled weight matrix.
paraphrase: Pipeline: (1) concat group → multiply by S → form S_g W_g; (2) compute Reff(g) via Eqs 1-2; (3) solve Eq. 7 to get [k1..kG]; (4) truncate top-k singular values for each scaled group.
preconditions: Per-group calibration; same matrix-type grouping (Q with Q, K with K, etc.)
consequences: Defines exact Stage 3 ordering

label: Figure 2
verbatim: We observe that WV consistently exhibits much larger Reff (which is often > 1000) than WQ, WK indicating that the information density is unevenly distributed across the attention layers.
paraphrase: Empirical: WV has far larger effective rank (>1000) than WQ, WK across LLaMA-7B groups.
consequences: Direct motivation for β rebalance Eqs 8-12

verbatim: assigning the number of retained singular values k solely based on the effective ranks of each type of matrix according to the Lagrangian method would be unfair to the WV matrices.
paraphrase: Lagrangian alone underweights WV; need ad-hoc β redistribution from Q,K to V.
consequences: Justifies the second stage rebalance

label: Eq. 8
paraphrase: Per-matrix-type rank lists from the Lagrangian step (example with G=4 for LLaMA-7B 32 layers / 8 layers per group).
preconditions: Lagrangian allocation Eq. 7 applied independently to Q, K, V
consequences: Inputs to β rebalance Eqs 9-12

label: Eq. 9
verbatim: L^Q_final-k = (1 − β) [k^Q_1, k^Q_2, k^Q_3, k^Q_4]
paraphrase: Scale Q-rank list by (1−β).
consequences: Removes β fraction of Q rank

label: Eq. 10
verbatim: L^K_final-k = (1 − β) [k^K_1, k^K_2, k^K_3, k^K_4]
paraphrase: Scale K-rank list by (1−β).
consequences: Removes β fraction of K rank

label: Eq. 11
paraphrase: Per-group transfer t = (β/G) · (sum of Q-ranks + sum of K-ranks). Here divisor 4 = G (number of groups in the example).
preconditions: G groups; the divisor is G in general (here G=4)
consequences: WARNING: the literal '/4' is example-specific to G=4; generic form is /G

label: Eq. 12
verbatim: L^V_final-k = [k^V_1 + t, k^V_2 + t, k^V_3 + t, k^V_4 + t]
paraphrase: Add the same t evenly to every group's V rank.
consequences: Final adjusted V-rank list

label: β
verbatim: The parameter β serves as a tunable hyperparameter, and we will provide a detailed analysis of its impact in the experimental section.
paraphrase: β is the only attention-rebalance tunable; the paper sweeps it in Table 5.
consequences: Spec must allow β configuration

label: §3.4 GQA
verbatim: On LLaMA-3-8B, the WK, WV projection matrices have dimensions of 4096 × 1024. When such matrices are horizontally concatenated within a group, the dimension expands severely and the matrix rank could be even larger than the rank of any individual matrix. Under a fixed compression ratio, the resulting SVD truncation will lead to a larger reconstruction error for the concatenated matrix than for compressing the original per-layer matrices separately.
paraphrase: GQA: WK/WV are 4096×1024; horizontal concat with n>1 inflates effective rank → fixed-ratio truncation hurts more than per-layer compression.
consequences: Spec must use n=1 for GQA models

label: §3.4 GQA Rule
verbatim: for models with grouped-query attentions, such as LLaMA-3, we set the group size as n = 1, and we use our proposed compression scheme that (i) dynamically adjusts the retained rank k of each layer according to its effective rank; (ii) reallocates a portion of the k budget from the WQ and WK matrices to the WV matrices.
paraphrase: GQA rule: n=1 + dynamic rank by Reff + β-rebalance Q,K → V.
consequences: Spec rule for LLaMA-3-8B and other GQA models

label: Table 2
paraphrase: Empirical degradation of Basis Sharing on LLaMA-3-8B as n increases.
consequences: Empirical evidence for n=1 rule

label: §4.1 Datasets
verbatim: For language modeling, we use three datasets: PTB, WikiText2, and C4. To evaluate the model's reasoning ability, we employ seven reasoning datasets: MathQA, PIQA, ARC-e, ARC-c, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, and OpenbookQA. The LM-Evaluation-Harness framework has been applied to test every reasoning task through a zero-shot setting.
paraphrase: Evaluation datasets: PTB+WikiText2+C4 for LM PPL; 7 reasoning tasks via lm-eval-harness zero-shot.
consequences: Spec eval should match these datasets

label: §4.1 Models
verbatim: we conduct comprehensive evaluations of D-Rank across multiple LLMs, including the LLaMA family (LLaMA-7B, LLaMA-13B, LLaMA-30B, LLaMA-2-7B, LLaMA-3-8B) and Mistral-7B
paraphrase: Models tested: LLaMA-{7,13,30}B, LLaMA-2-7B, LLaMA-3-8B, Mistral-7B.
consequences: Reference model set

label: §4.1 Hardware/Precision
verbatim: All experiments are conducted on two NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. The LLaMA-30B model is implemented in FP16 precision, while all other models utilize FP32 precision. We use FP64 to maintain the computational precision of matrix S.
paraphrase: 2× A100 80GB; FP32 weights for ≤13B, FP16 for 30B; FP64 for whitening matrix S.
consequences: CRITICAL: FP64 for S — H200 spec must preserve FP64 whitening or document deviation

label: §4.1 Calibration
verbatim: Matrix S is derived from 256 samples of WikiText-2 with a sequence length of 2048.
paraphrase: Calibration: 256 samples × 2048 tokens from WikiText-2 used to compute X⊤X / S.
consequences: Spec must use exactly 256 × 2048 calibration

label: §4.1 Hi-Ratio Update
verbatim: Note that when the compression ratio is 40% or more, accumulated compression errors lead to significant inter-layer input deviation from original values. We adaptively update the downstream layer weights using the deviated inputs, similar to the method used in SVD-LLM.
paraphrase: At ratio≥40%, downstream layer weights are adaptively updated using compressed-layer outputs (SVD-LLM-style closed-form update).
consequences: Spec must apply downstream-update at high ratios

label: §4.1 Grouping
verbatim: matrices like WQ, WK, WV, Wup, and Wgate in MHA-based models are grouped and compressed in our experiments when n > 1, while Wdown and WO are not grouped.
paraphrase: Grouping rule (n>1): group {WQ, WK, WV, Wup, Wgate}; do NOT group {Wdown, WO}.
consequences: CRITICAL: spec must NOT group Wdown/WO; only the 5 listed types

label: Table 3
paraphrase: Headline LLaMA-7B PPL/zero-shot at 20–50% compression ratios, calibration WikiText-2, n=2.
consequences: Reference numbers for spec sanity checks

label: Table 4
verbatim: LLaMA-3-8B @20% D-Rank: WikiText-2=13.68 C4=44.87 Openb=0.27 ARC_e=0.68 WinoG=0.67 HellaS=0.43 ARC_c=0.33 PIQA=0.71 MathQA=0.28 Avg=0.48 (Original Wiki=6.14 C4=9.47 Avg=0.55; Basis Sharing n=5 Wiki=17.09 C4=60.08 Avg=0.46)
paraphrase: LLaMA-3-8B 20% headline. D-Rank reduces C4 PPL by ≥15 vs all baselines.
consequences: Reference for GQA performance

label: Table 5 β-sweep
paraphrase: β=0.3–0.4 consistently optimal; redistribution always beats Basis Sharing baseline.
consequences: Recommended β default range 0.3–0.35

label: §4.2 β choice
verbatim: an appropriate choice of β significantly improves performance compared with the Basis Sharing baseline. In particular, β = 0.3–0.4 consistently yields the lowest PPL across different settings.
paraphrase: Recommended β range: 0.3–0.4.
consequences: Spec default β should fall in 0.3–0.4

label: §4.2 LoRA
verbatim: Our LoRA fine-tuning settings include lora_r = 8, lora_alpha = 32, and learning_rate = 1e − 4, and we use default settings for all other hyperparameters in the Hugging Face PEFT. Each compressed model is fine tuned with WikiText-2 training dataset for two epochs.
paraphrase: LoRA recovery: r=8, alpha=32, lr=1e-4, 2 epochs WikiText-2, HF PEFT defaults.
consequences: Spec LoRA recovery defaults if applied

label: Table 6
verbatim: 20% PPL D-Rank: LLaMA-7B 7.45 / LLaMA-2-7B 7.51 / Mistral-7B 7.41. SVD-LLM: 7.94/8.50/10.21. Basis Sharing: 7.74/7.70/7.57.
paraphrase: 20% PPL on three 7B-class models — D-Rank wins all.
consequences: Cross-model robustness reference

label: Table 7
verbatim: LLaMA scales 20% PPL D-Rank: 7B=7.45, 13B=6.30, 30B=5.33. SVD-LLM 7.94/6.61/5.63. Basis Sharing 7.75/6.47/5.47.
paraphrase: Scaling: D-Rank advantage holds 7B → 30B.
consequences: Reference for spec scale validation

label: Table 8
paraphrase: Calibration on C4 — D-Rank still best across n=2..5.
consequences: Out-of-distribution calibration robustness

label: Figure 4 Throughput
verbatim: at 50% compression, our approach reaches nearly 2,200 tokens/sec, which exceeds both SVD-LLM and Basis Sharing and offers more than a 60% gain over the dense model.
paraphrase: Throughput: ~2200 tok/s @50%, >60% over dense.
consequences: Hardware claim spec may inherit

label: Eq. 13
verbatim: min_{k1,...,kG} ℓ_total = Σ_{g=1}^G Reff(g)/k_g  s.t.  Σ_{g=1}^G k_g ω = Tbudget
paraphrase: Restatement of the optimization problem (matches Eq. 3).
consequences: Setup for closed-form derivation

label: Eq. 14
verbatim: F({k_g}, λ) = Σ_{g=1}^G Reff(g)/k_g + λ ( Σ_{g=1}^G k_g ω − Tbudget )
paraphrase: Lagrangian (matches Eq. 4).
consequences: First-order conditions Eq. 15

label: Eq. 15
verbatim: ∂F/∂k_g = − Reff(g)/k_g^2 + λ ω = 0  ⇒  k_g = sqrt(Reff(g) / (λ ω))
paraphrase: Stationary k_g (matches Eq. 5 plus explicit solve).
consequences: Yields proportionality

label: Eq. 16
verbatim: k_g ∝ sqrt(Reff(g)) / sqrt(ω)
paraphrase: Proportionality form (matches Eq. 6).
consequences: Constant C resolved via budget

label: Eq. 17
verbatim: Σ_{g=1}^G k_g ω = Σ_{g=1}^G ( C sqrt(Reff(g))/sqrt(ω) ) ω = C Σ_{g=1}^G sqrt(Reff(g) ω) = Tbudget
paraphrase: Substitute proportional form into budget constraint.
consequences: Solves C

label: Eq. 18
verbatim: C = Tbudget / Σ_{j=1}^G sqrt(Reff(j) ω)
paraphrase: Proportionality constant.
consequences: Yields Eq. 19

label: Eq. 19
verbatim: k_g = ( Tbudget / Σ_{j=1}^G sqrt(Reff(j) ω) ) · ( sqrt(Reff(g)) / sqrt(ω) )
paraphrase: Final closed-form rank allocation (formal restatement of Eq. 7). This is the canonical D-Rank allocation formula.
preconditions: Reff(g) from Eq. 2; ω = d1 + n d2
consequences: AUTHORITATIVE form of D-Rank Eq. 7 — spec must implement this byte-equivalent

label: §A.3 Interpretation
verbatim: Equation 16-19 show that groups with larger information content Reff(g) receive higher ranks, whereas groups with higher parameter cost ω receive fewer ranks, all under the fixed budget Tbudget.
paraphrase: Interpretive note on Eqs 16-19.
consequences: Confirms direction of allocation

label: §A.2 Seeds
verbatim: the performance of both SVD-LLM and Basis Sharing fluctuates with the choice of seed, while D-Rank consistently achieves lower PPL across all settings. For instance, at seed 13, D-Rank obtains 7.45 compared to 7.9 for SVD-LLM and 7.7 for Basis Sharing
paraphrase: Seed robustness: D-Rank PPL stable across seeds 13/42/512/1024.
preconditions: Seeds for sample selection in calibration
consequences: Seed has limited effect on final D-Rank

label: §3.2.2 ω derivation
verbatim: For a shared basis, this is calculated as ω = d1 + n d2, where n is the number of layers in the group.
paraphrase: When n=1 (no shared basis grouping), ω = d1 + d2 — the standard SVD parameter cost per rank.
consequences: Spec n=1 path: per-layer ω = d_in + d_out

label: §3.1 Concat
verbatim: we can first concatenate matrices within the same group horizontally
paraphrase: Horizontal (column-wise) concatenation, not vertical — final matrix is d1 × (n·d2).
preconditions: Same row dim d1 across n layers in group
consequences: Spec must use horizontal concat (column dim grows)

label: §3.1 Decomposition
verbatim: each column of W(i) is expressed as a linear combination of k shared basis vectors: W(i)_:,j ≈ Σ_{m=1}^k B_:,m C(i)_{m,j}
paraphrase: Per-layer matrix is approximated as B C^(i) where B is shared (d1×k) and C^(i) is layer-specific (k×d2).
consequences: Inference path: shared basis multiply once per group + per-layer C

label: §3.2.2 Loss assumption
verbatim: the error is inversely proportional to the allocated rank and proportional to the effective rank.
paraphrase: Modeling assumption underlying Eq. 3: ℓ_g(k_g) = Reff(g)/k_g.
preconditions: Heuristic, not theoretically derived from reconstruction error bound
consequences: Different from a true Eckart–Young bound; a known modeling simplification

label: Question 1
verbatim: How does the information content in weights vary across layers? What metric should we use to quantify it, and how can it guide adaptive rank allocation for model compression?
paraphrase: Research Q1 — motivation for effective rank metric.
consequences: Frames §3.2

label: Question 2
verbatim: Do different weight matrices in attention layers, especially WQ, WK, and WV, contain different levels of information, and should non-uniform ranks be allocated for attention layer compression?
paraphrase: Research Q2 — motivation for β rebalance.
consequences: Frames §3.3

label: §1 Contributions
verbatim: • We propose D-Rank... • We introduce a novel metric, effective rank... Lagrangian multiplier-based framework that dynamically allocates ranks across grouped layers... • Through effective rank analysis, we discover that the effective rank distribution among the attentions matrices is highly unbalanced: WQ, WK have lower effective rank (less information) than WV matrix. To address this issue, we propose a reallocation strategy that transfers part of the preserved rank budget from WQ, WK to WV. • Moreover, we analyze the reason why the performance of latest models (e.g., LLaMA-3) with grouped-query attention degrades
paraphrase: Four contributions: (1) D-Rank framework, (2) Reff metric + Lagrangian, (3) Q,K→V β rebalance, (4) GQA fix (n=1).
consequences: Paper scope summary

label: §3.1 S def
verbatim: SS⊤ = cholesky(X⊤X)
paraphrase: S is the lower Cholesky factor of X⊤X (i.e., S S⊤ = X⊤X). Note: NOT (X⊤X)^{1/2}.
consequences: CRITICAL: spec must use Cholesky factor, not symmetric square root
